page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.ce4.xlarge is an x86_64 architecture server instance powered by a dedicated Intel Xeon E5-2667 v4 processor. This CPU features 4 physical cores and 4 vCPUs running at a clock speed of 3.2 GHz, supported by a 25 MB L3 cache. The instance is configured with 32.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and it operates with a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates that the ecs.ce4.xlarge exhibits modest performance characteristics. Its single-core and multi-core CPU performance, measured via stress-ng and Geekbench, falls into the bottom 25% tier. Memory bandwidth tests show a similar pattern, with uncached read and write speeds of 22.44 GB/sec and 14.99 GB/sec respectively. However, the instance achieves average performance in single-threaded gzip compression at 25.96 GB/sec and single-threaded bzip3 decompression at 9.53 GB/sec. Multi-threaded bzip3 decompression is a notable bottleneck, registering in the bottom 10% tier.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the ecs.ce4.xlarge offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio on dedicated legacy Intel hardware, making it a viable option for memory-intensive workloads that do not require cutting-edge compute speeds. Given the lack of local storage and lower CPU benchmark scores, this instance is best suited for memory-optimized tasks such as caching layers, lightweight database operations, text processing, and basic web serving where memory capacity is prioritized over raw processing throughput.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.ce4.xlarge is an x86_64 server instance featuring a dedicated Intel Xeon E5-2667 v4 processor with 4 physical cores, 4 vCPUs, and a 3.2 GHz clock speed. It is equipped with 32.0 GB of RAM, providing a high 8.0 GB of memory per core, but lacks local storage and GPU accelerators. Network bandwidth is rated at a baseline of 2 Gbps. Performance benchmarks place the instance primarily in the lower tiers, with weak single-core and multi-core CPU scores, though it maintains average single-threaded compression and text processing capabilities. Given its high memory-to-vCPU ratio and dedicated CPU allocation, the ecs.ce4.xlarge is qualitatively cost-effective for memory-sensitive workloads that do not demand high compute performance, such as caching, lightweight database operations, and text processing.
